title: Geometry
objective: Students will explore geometry concepts including Types of angles, Types of angles (age 8+), Understanding angles (age 8+) and more.

steps.content: Today we're going to learn about Types of angles. Get ready for an adventure!
Identify acute and obtuse angles; compare and order angles up to two right angles by size
Time to try this out: Classify given angles as acute, right, or obtuse
If you looks at the hands on a clock showing different times, can they tell you which angle between the hands is acute (sharper than a right angle) and which is obtuse (wider)?
Great job! Today we learned about Types of angles. Classify given angles as acute, right, or obtuse Order four angles from smallest to largest by visual comparison
Have you ever thought about Types of angles (age 8+)? Let's find out together!

Use and interpret standard geometric diagram conventions: mark right angles with a small square, equal lengths with single or double tick marks, and equal angles with arc marks; label angles in three-letter notation (∠ABC) and individual angles with a single letter or number; draw diagrams showing angles at a point, angles on a straight line, and angles inside polygons with these conventions; read diagrams with these marks to identify given information and find unknown values
Let me show you something cool. Mark a right angle with a small square symbol in a diagram and explain what it means

If you's maths diagram shows a small square in one corner and matching arc marks on two other angles, can they tell you what each mark means — and use them to figure out a missing angle without re-measuring?

Great job! Today we learned about Types of angles (age 8+). Mark a right angle with a small square symbol in a diagram and explain what it means Use tick marks to show equal lengths in a shape and double tick marks for a second pair of equal sides
Let's discover the amazing world of Understanding angles (age 8+)!

Understand that shapes in different categories may share attributes defining a larger category; classify quadrilaterals (rhombuses, rectangles, squares) and draw examples of quadrilaterals not in those subcategories
Let's practice together! Explain that a square is a special rectangle and also a special rhombus
If you show you a square, a rectangle, and a rhombus, can they explain what makes them all quadrilaterals — and what makes each one different from the others?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Understanding angles (age 8+). Explain that a square is a special rectangle and also a special rhombus Sort shapes into a Venn diagram: quadrilaterals vs rectangles vs squares
Let's explore First Quadrant Coordinates! Have you ever wondered about this?
Describe positions on a 2-D grid as coordinates in the first quadrant
Let's practice together! Read the coordinates of a point on a grid as (3, 5)
If you is playing a grid-based treasure map game, can they correctly say where the treasure is using two numbers — like "three across and four up" — from the corner?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about First Quadrant Coordinates. Read the coordinates of a point on a grid as (3, 5) Explain that the first number is the horizontal distance and the second is the vertical distance
Today we're going to learn about Coordinates (age 8+). Get ready for an adventure!
Plot specified points on a coordinate grid and draw sides to complete a given polygon
Let's practice together! Plot points (1,1), (1,4), (5,4), (5,1) and join to make a rectangle
If you is given three coordinate pairs on a grid, can they plot each point and then connect the dots to draw the correct triangle or rectangle?
Great job! Today we learned about Coordinates (age 8+). Plot points (1,1), (1,4), (5,4), (5,1) and join to make a rectangle Given three vertices of a square, plot the fourth vertex
Today we're going to learn about Transformations on a grid. Get ready for an adventure!

Represent and carry out geometric transformations on squared paper or a coordinate grid: reflections (in horizontal, vertical, and diagonal mirror lines, including the axes), translations (described as a vector or as left/right/up/down moves), and rotations (90° or 180° about a stated centre point); describe each transformation precisely using the correct language; identify which transformation maps one shape onto its image by comparing position, orientation, and size
Let me show you something cool. Reflect a shape in a given mirror line on a grid and label the new coordinates

If you sees a shape and its mirror image on squared paper, can they draw in the exact mirror line, and describe the reflection precisely — including where the line of reflection is — without just saying 'it flipped'?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Transformations on a grid. Reflect a shape in a given mirror line on a grid and label the new coordinates Translate a shape by a given number of squares horizontally and vertically and describe the movement
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Nets of 3-D Shapes!

Identify, draw, and interpret nets of common 3-D shapes — cubes, cuboids, triangular prisms, and square-based pyramids — by predicting which 3-D shape a given flat arrangement of faces will fold into, checking whether a net will close completely, and sketching a net from a description or 3-D model; understand the relationship between the number of faces and the structure of the net
Let's practice together! Draw the net of a cube, cuboid, or triangular prism and fold it mentally to identify which faces connect
If you is given a flat T-shaped piece of card with six squares, can they tell you — before folding — that it makes a cube, and point out which square will become each face?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Nets of 3-D Shapes. Draw the net of a cube, cuboid, or triangular prism and fold it mentally to identify which faces connect Build a 3-D shape from its net and check that all faces, edges, and vertices match
Let's explore Describing Movements! Have you ever wondered about this?
Describe movements between positions as translations of a given unit to the left/right and up/down
Let me show you something cool. Describe moving from (2,3) to (5,3) as 3 units to the right
If a shape is at position (2, 3) on a grid and you tell you to move it four squares right and two squares up, can they work out its new position without drawing it first?
Great job! Today we learned about Describing Movements. Describe moving from (2,3) to (5,3) as 3 units to the right Translate a shape 4 units right and 2 units up and state the new coordinates
Let's discover the amazing world of 2-D shapes (age 8+)!
Identify lines of symmetry in 2-D shapes presented in different orientations; recognise line-symmetric figures and draw lines of symmetry
Let me show you something cool. Find all lines of symmetry in a rectangle, square, and equilateral triangle
If you is given a picture of a butterfly or a star shape, can they draw the line (or lines) where you could fold it exactly in half so both sides match?

You did it! Here's what we explored about 2-D shapes (age 8+): Find all lines of symmetry in a rectangle, square, and equilateral triangle Determine whether a given shape has a line of symmetry when rotated
Let's explore Lines of symmetry! Have you ever wondered about this?
Complete a simple symmetric figure with respect to a specific line of symmetry
Let's practice together! Given half a butterfly shape and a mirror line, complete the other half on a grid
If you show you half a shape drawn on squared paper with a mirror line marked, can they draw the missing half so the whole shape is perfectly symmetrical?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Lines of symmetry. Given half a butterfly shape and a mirror line, complete the other half on a grid Complete a symmetric pattern on squared paper with a vertical line of symmetry
